select_when: An incumbent option may be surviving because changing feels costly rather than because it is actually superior.
Defaults, routines, or prior commitments are shaping judgment quietly and the team needs to separate real switching costs from inertia.
A proposal to stay the course sounds prudent but may be disguising loss aversion, sunk costs, or unchallenged precedent.
avoid_when: Every legacy choice is being treated as bad simply because it is old, rather than asking whether the current default still outperforms the alternatives.
The desire to break inertia is turning into novelty-seeking change without enough baseline evidence that the new option is actually better.

curation_notes.summary: Normalized the model around default testing and inertia versus real switching cost, not donor lists of generic assumption-checking cases.
donor_drops: Dropped donor broad strategy and AI-governance examples because they widened the model beyond default-preservation structure.
Dropped donor higher-order relation fields to keep the contract frozen.
open_questions: The reasoning-types field is a reviewed taxonomy choice over a source that is more explicit about bias structure than about reasoning labels.

select_when.note: The raw file explicitly states the incumbent-versus-inertia activation logic; donor drafts were narrowed into those same structural triggers.

avoid_when.note: Both anti-activation conditions are directly supported by explicit raw danger lines.

input_type.note: The model clearly applies to decisions that preserve an incumbent default, but the typed label is reviewed normalization.

output_type.note: The raw model tests whether the default deserves to survive; the output label compresses that review function into an operational artifact type.

reasoning_types.note: The model is diagnostic and counterfactual in use, but the taxonomy labels remain reviewed normalization.
